hypothesis: At gate=25% Erlang-C, protecting c=1 ticks with on-demand (0 spot) eliminates the BurstGPT spot-interruption cliff and yields higher goodput/$ than AMCSG gate=12.5%.
reason: The simulation already contains a max(1,...) guard preventing c_effective=0. C1PGS has identical effective capacity to GSF at c=1. SLA violations at gate=25% come from Erlang-C over-optimism, not spot interruptions. On BurstGPT, OD cost at c=1 ($2.00/hr) exceeds GSF c=2 spot ($1.60/hr) at gate=12.5%, making C1PGS more expensive AND SLA-unsafe.
constitutional_status: NOT A PRODUCT CLAIM — hypothesis falsified, not merged as optimizer improvement

azure_llm_2024.notes: C1PGS raw goodput/$ is higher (+2.21%) but 5 more SLA violations vs AMCSG. SLA safety criterion FAILS. Gate=25% Erlang-C too optimistic on 5 ticks.

burstgpt_hf.notes: C1PGS costs MORE (+7.8%) and serves fewer SLA-safe requests (-5). OD at c=1 ($2.00/hr) > AMCSG GSF c=2 spot ($1.60/hr). Hypothesis falsified on BurstGPT.

root_cause_analysis.mechanism_assumed: spot interruptions at c=1 ticks causing c_effective=0
root_cause_analysis.mechanism_actual: simulation guard max(1, c_demand+survived) prevents c_effective=0; violations come from Erlang-C over-optimism at gate=25%
root_cause_analysis.cost_error_burstgpt: OD at c=1 ($2.00/hr) > GSF c=2 all-spot at gate=12.5% SLA=30s ($1.60/hr); BurstGPT longer SLA allows lower c at gate=12.5% than Azure
root_cause_analysis.next_direction: Address Erlang-C conservatism gap directly: SOTSS-MIN already does this (oracle refines c_schedule); C1PGS not needed
